response:
# Decision: Ship the free-tier cap at 1,000 requests/day (Option A)

## What the data actually supports

The CSV confirms the memo's ~100 req/key/day average and shows it's remarkably stable — the requests-per-active-key ratio ranges only 94–106 across all 30 days (e.g. 2026-06-19: 45 keys / 4,770 requests = 106; 2026-06-03: 40 keys / 4,020 = 100.5). That tight, low-variance ratio is the only real signal here.

It is not, however, a per-key distribution. The CSV is a daily aggregate of `active_keys` and `total_requests` — there's no way to tell from it whether that ~100/day average reflects ~40 uniformly-light users or a handful of heavy users pulling the mean up while most idle far below. Notably, the memo's claim that "busiest days stay well under a per-key average of 250" isn't verifiable from this file at all — the max observed daily ratio here is 106, not 250 — which means even the memo's own supporting evidence for headroom rests on data not in front of me.

## Decision

**Cap the free tier at 1,000 requests/day at launch**, matching the built and load-tested rig. Do not set a lower cap (Option B or C) at this time.

## Rationale

- Billing, the Pro tier, and the free cap all go live simultaneously. A cap that binds on day one and turns out wrong is inseparable from "the price is wrong" or "the product isn't there yet" in the resulting churn/conversion data — there's no way to isolate the cap's effect from everything else that changed at once.
- Quillmark is consumed programmatically, not through a UI. Free users who are integrated into a pipeline get *no signal* that they're near a wall until requests silently start failing. Getting the initial number wrong is far more costly for an API than for a UI product, and there's no support team to catch it early.
- There is no per-key distribution in hand. Setting a tight cap (B) without knowing whether usage is uniform or skewed risks hard-stopping exactly the small set of users building something real on Quillmark — the users most likely to become paying customers or advocates — while doing nothing to the median idle key.
- 1,000/day costs nothing extra to ship (already built and load-tested) and guarantees zero existing free user is broken at the single riskiest moment (simultaneous billing + tier + cap launch).

## Load-bearing assumption (stated per instructions)

**Assumption:** Free users staying comfortably free for the next several weeks is an acceptable cost — the cap does not need to do conversion work *at launch*; monetization pressure can be introduced later, deliberately, once real signal exists.

**Failure class I am accepting if this assumption is false:** forgone/delayed revenue. If in fact the business needs the cap to be actively converting users from day one — because revenue is urgent, or because tightening the cap later reads as a bait-and-switch to users who onboarded under a generous limit — then this decision is wrong, and Option B (accept the churn risk, take the conversion swing immediately) would have been the better call.

## What would change this number later

I'll revisit and tighten the cap once two things exist that don't exist today:
1. **Per-key distribution data** from the live dashboard (not the historical aggregate CSV) — enough to tell whether usage is uniform or concentrated in a few heavy keys, so a new cap targets the right population instead of guessing.
2. **Actual Pro conversion data** — real upgrade/churn behavior once Pro has been live for a few weeks, so a tightened cap is calibrated against observed willingness-to-pay rather than the untested "usage ≈ willingness-to-pay" assumption that Option B was built on.

Any future tightening will be shipped as its own isolated, communicated change — not bundled with another simultaneous product shift — specifically so that if churn follows, it can be attributed to the cap and not confused with something else.
